name: Watchers Angels
description: The concept of Watchers Angels, often referred to as 'Gregori' or 'Irin', originates primarily from ancient Jewish and Christian texts, notably the Book of Enoch. Proponents believe these Watchers were a group of angelic beings tasked by God to observe humanity. However, some Watchers are said to have disobeyed God by descending to Earth, interbreeding with human women, and teaching forbidden knowledge. This union is believed to have resulted in the Nephilim, giants known for their strength and wickedness. These narratives are interpreted by some as explanations for the origins of evil, advanced ancient technologies, and pre-diluvian civilizations. Evidence cited includes passages in the Book of Enoch, fragments of the Dead Sea Scrolls, and interpretations of biblical verses. Some proponents also link the Watchers to extraterrestrial beings or ancient astronauts, suggesting they were not angels but advanced beings from another planet. Mainstream scholarly perspective views the Book of Enoch as apocryphal literature, not canonical scripture, and its narratives as symbolic or allegorical rather than literal historical accounts. Scholars often interpret these stories within the context of ancient Near Eastern mythology and cosmology, viewing them as attempts to explain the origins of evil and the challenges to divine order. The narratives are not generally considered historical or scientific fact by mainstream academics.
subtitle: Fallen Angels and Forbidden Knowledge

claims.claim: Watchers Angels were celestial beings tasked with observing humanity.
claims.evidence: Book of Enoch, Dead Sea Scrolls fragments referencing Watchers.
claims.criticism: Mainstream scholarship considers the Book of Enoch apocryphal; these texts are not considered historically accurate.

claims.claim: Some Watchers descended to Earth and interbred with human women, creating the Nephilim.
claims.evidence: Genesis 6:1-4 (interpreted differently by different theological perspectives), Book of Enoch's detailed accounts of the Watchers' actions.
claims.criticism: Mainstream interpretation of Genesis 6:1-4 often suggests the 'sons of God' were powerful human rulers, not angels. The Nephilim are seen as metaphorical figures rather than literal giants.

claims.claim: The Watchers taught humanity forbidden knowledge, leading to corruption and a great flood.
claims.evidence: Book of Enoch describes the Watchers teaching metallurgy, astrology, and other arts.
claims.criticism: Scholars see these narratives as symbolic explanations for the origins of evil and societal corruption, drawing parallels to other ancient myths about the introduction of civilization and its consequences.

keyFigures.name: R.H. Charles
keyFigures.role: Translator and commentator on the Book of Enoch

keyFigures.name: Zecharia Sitchin
keyFigures.role: Promoted the idea of the Watchers as extraterrestrial beings (part of the Annunaki)

keyFigures.name: George Nickelsburg
keyFigures.role: Scholar specializing in Jewish Apocalyptic Literature, including the Book of Enoch

timeline.event: Earliest sections of the Book of Enoch are believed to have been written.
References to Enochian traditions appear in the New Testament (e.g., Jude 14-15).
Interest in the Book of Enoch and the Watchers Angels resurfaces in esoteric and occult circles.
Theories linking Watchers to extraterrestrial beings gain popularity in alternative history and conspiracy circles.

sources.author: George W.E. Nickelsburg
sources.work: 1 Enoch: A Commentary on the Book of 1 Enoch
sources.title: George W.E. Nickelsburg

sources.author: John J. Collins
sources.work: The Apocalyptic Imagination: An Introduction to Jewish Apocalyptic Literature
sources.title: John J. Collins
